title: Effects of mobile phone distraction on pedestrians' crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection: An outdoor experimental study
research_problem: Investigates how mobile phone distractions (music, phone conversation, and text) affect pedestrian crossing behavior and visual attention allocation at a signalized intersection
method_summary: Outdoor-environment experiment with 28 college student pedestrians using two HD videos and Tobii Pro Glasses 2 eye tracker to record crossing behavior and visual attention under four conditions: undistracted, music distraction, phone conversation distraction, and text distraction
datasets: 28 college students at signalized intersection in Hefei, China
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior under mobile phone distraction

main_claims.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
Pedestrians distracted by music initiate crossing later, increase pupil diameter, and reduce scanning frequency and fixation points toward traffic signal area
Pedestrians distracted by phone conversation cross more slowly, direct fewer fixation points to right traffic area, and spend less fixation time on left traffic area
Pedestrians distracted by texting look left and right less often and switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment
Eye movement data captured by eye tracker provides more accurate determination of visual attention allocation compared to video data alone

limitations.text: Sample limited to college students (ages 17-25), may not represent overall pedestrian population
Participants restricted from self-regulation and behavioral adaptation in experiment, may not reflect daily life behavior
Data collected at one specific signalized intersection with very low traffic volume, may not generalize to complex intersections
Use of unfamiliar phone and mental arithmetic tasks may not conform to participants' daily life mobile phone use patterns
Only three types of mobile phone distractions studied (music, conversation, text), other tasks like mobile internet not examined

future_work: Extend research to more varied sample populations beyond college students
Test in other environments such as non-signalized intersections or intersections with high traffic volume
Study other mobile phone tasks such as mobile internet for fuller understanding of distraction effects
Investigate design elements and driver regulations to protect distracted pedestrians
notes: Outdoor experimental study using Tobii Pro Glasses 2 for eye tracking; found text distraction most harmful, followed by phone conversation and music; research funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China
